1:
"And Abram went up out of Egypt, he, and his wife, and all that he had, and Lot with him, into the south." The witnesses note Abram's going up out of Egypt back to Canaan. On "Abram went up out of Egypt," the grammatical voices note the return. Gill and Henry note that "Abram went up out of Egypt, he, and his wife, and all that he had, and Lot with him" — Abram returned from Egypt (going "up" toward the higher land of Canaan) with his wife Sarai (restored, 12:19), his nephew Lot, and all his possessions; the patriarch, delivered from Egypt, came back to the land of promise; the return from the place of his fault and trial.

On "he, and his wife, and all that he had," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the household. Calvin and Gill note that Abram came up with Sarai (restored to him), Lot, and all his substance (increased in Egypt, 12:16); the household of the patriarch returning intact; God's mercy bringing him safely out, with his wife and goods, despite his fault.

On "into the south," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the place: "into the south" — Abram came into the south (the Negev, the southern part of Canaan, where he had been before going to Egypt, 12:9); the patriarch retracing his steps, returning to the land of promise and to his worship (vv.3-4); the pilgrim back in Canaan, to renew his sojourn. The verse displays Abram's going up out of Egypt back to Canaan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the return — "Abram went up out of Egypt, he, and his wife, and all that he had, and Lot with him," Abram returning from Egypt with his household (Gill, Henry); "he, and his wife, and all that he had," Sarai restored and the substance increased (12:16, 19, Calvin, Gill); "into the south," the patriarch back in the Negev (12:9, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the returning Abram. The verse marks the going-up: out of Egypt. The verse displays the household: with him. The verse displays the south: returned to. The verse displays the promise: land regained. The return displays Abram coming back to Canaan from Egypt. The verse displays the Abram: returned. The verse displays the wife: restored. The verse displays the Canaan: regained. Abram's going up out of Egypt back to Canaan displays the return of the delivered patriarch: Abram went up out of Egypt with his wife, Lot, and all he had, into the south — the patriarch, delivered and restored, returning to the land of promise and his worship. The verse displays the returned Abram, very rich (v.2).
2:
"And Abram was very rich in cattle, in silver, and in gold." The very brief verse notes Abram's great riches. The grammatical voices note the wealth. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abram was very rich in cattle, in silver, and in gold" — Abram had become very wealthy, abounding in livestock (cattle, the chief wealth of the pastoral patriarch), silver, and gold; his riches, increased in Egypt (12:16); the great substance of the patriarch, a mark of God's blessing on him.

On "very rich in cattle," the witnesses note the pastoral wealth. Gill notes that Abram's wealth was chiefly in cattle (flocks and herds), the substance of a nomadic herdsman; his great flocks and herds; the wealth that would soon require the parting from Lot (vv.6-7, the land not bearing them both).

On "in silver, and in gold," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the riches: "in silver, and in gold" — Abram also had silver and gold; the patriarch was rich in precious metals as well as livestock; the blessing of God on Abram in temporal things; yet his true riches were the promises of God, and he remained a pilgrim and stranger (Hebrews 11:9), not setting his heart on his wealth. The verse displays Abram's great riches.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the wealth — "Abram was very rich in cattle, in silver, and in gold," Abram's great riches in livestock and precious metals (Keil, Gill); "very rich in cattle," the pastoral wealth that would require the parting from Lot (vv.6-7, Gill); "in silver, and in gold," the blessing of God in temporal things (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rich Abram. The verse marks the riches: very great. The verse displays the cattle: abundant. The verse displays the silver: and gold. The verse displays the blessing: temporal. The wealth displays Abram abounding in substance. The verse displays the Abram: rich. The verse displays the cattle: many. The verse displays the gold: his. Abram's great riches display the blessing of God on the patriarch: Abram was very rich in cattle, silver, and gold — the great substance of the pilgrim patriarch, increased in Egypt, a mark of God's blessing, yet his true riches the promises of God. The verse displays the rich Abram, returning to Bethel (vv.3-4).
3:
"And he went on his journeys from the south even to Bethel, unto the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Bethel and Hai;" The brief verse notes Abram's return to Bethel, the place of his former altar. The grammatical voices note the journey. Gill and Wesley note that "he went on his journeys from the south even to Bethel, unto the place where his tent had been at the beginning, between Bethel and Hai" — Abram traveled by stages from the Negev back to Bethel, to the very place where he had pitched his tent and built an altar before going to Egypt (12:8); the patriarch returning to the place of his former worship; retracing his steps to where he had called on the LORD.

On "unto the place where his tent had been at the beginning," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the return to worship. Calvin and Gill note that Abram returned to "the place where his tent had been at the beginning" — to the spot of his earlier altar and worship (12:8); the patriarch, after the trial and fault of Egypt, returned to the place and the practice of his devotion; the renewal of his worship after his lapse; the believer returning to his first works.

On "between Bethel and Hai," the grammatical-historical note the place: "between Bethel and Hai (Ai)" — the place of Abram's former encampment and altar (12:8); the patriarch came back to where he had worshiped, to renew his calling on the name of the LORD (v.4); the return to the altar marks the restoration of his communion with God after Egypt. The verse displays Abram's return to Bethel, the place of his former altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the journey — "he went on his journeys from the south even to Bethel, unto the place where his tent had been at the beginning," Abram returning to Bethel, his former place (12:8, Gill, Wesley); "the place where his tent had been at the beginning," the spot of his earlier altar and worship (Calvin, Gill); "between Bethel and Hai," the place of his former encampment (12:8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the returning Abram. The verse marks the journey: to Bethel. The verse displays the place: his former tent. The verse displays the altar: his first. The verse displays the worship: renewed. The return displays Abram coming back to the place of his worship. The verse displays the Abram: returning. The verse displays the Bethel: regained. The verse displays the altar: revisited. Abram's return to Bethel, the place of his former altar, displays the renewal of his worship: Abram came back to Bethel, to the place of his tent and altar at the beginning (12:8) — the patriarch, after Egypt, returning to the place and practice of his devotion. The verse displays the returned Abram, calling on the LORD (v.4).
4:
"Unto the place of the altar, which he had made there at the first: and there Abram called on the name of the LORD." The very brief verse notes Abram's renewed worship at the altar. The grammatical voices note the altar. Gill notes that "unto the place of the altar, which he had made there at the first: and there Abram called on the name of the LORD" — Abram came to the place of the altar he had built at the first (12:8), and there renewed his worship, calling on the name of the LORD; the patriarch resuming his public worship of God at his former altar; the restoration of his devotion after Egypt.

On "the place of the altar, which he had made there at the first," the witnesses note the return. Gill notes that Abram returned to "the place of the altar... at the first" — to the altar of his earlier worship (12:8); the patriarch, after his lapse and deliverance, came back to the altar; the believer renewing his worship at the place of his former communion.

On "there Abram called on the name of the LORD," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the worship: "there Abram called on the name of the LORD" — Abram again worshiped and prayed, calling on the LORD (as at the first, 12:8); the public profession and worship renewed; the patriarch, humbled by his fault in Egypt, returned to call on God; the restoration of communion and worship; the believer's return to prayer and praise. The verse displays Abram's renewed worship at the altar.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the altar — "unto the place of the altar, which he had made there at the first," Abram returning to his former altar (12:8, Gill); "the place of the altar... at the first," the altar of his earlier worship (Gill); "there Abram called on the name of the LORD," the renewed worship and prayer (12:8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the worshiping Abram. The verse marks the altar: his first. The verse displays the worship: renewed. The verse displays the LORD: called upon. The verse displays the communion: restored. The worship displays Abram renewing his devotion at the altar. The verse displays the Abram: worshiping. The verse displays the altar: revisited. The verse displays the prayer: renewed. Abram's renewed worship at the altar displays the restoration of his devotion: Abram came to the place of his first altar and called on the name of the LORD — the patriarch, after Egypt, renewing his worship and prayer at the place of his former communion. The verse displays the worshiping Abram, with Lot's increasing flocks (v.5).
5:
"And Lot also, which went with Abram, had flocks, and herds, and tents." The brief verse notes Lot's own wealth in flocks, herds, and tents. The grammatical voices note Lot's substance. Gill and Henry note that "Lot also, which went with Abram, had flocks, and herds, and tents" — Lot, Abram's nephew, who had accompanied him, also had become wealthy, possessing his own flocks, herds, and tents; the prosperity of Lot, sharing (perhaps under God's blessing on Abram) in the increase; the substance of Lot that, with Abram's, would prove too great for the land (v.6).

On "had flocks, and herds, and tents," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the prosperity. Henry and Gill note that Lot had flocks, herds, and tents — the wealth of a pastoral household; Lot prospered alongside Abram; the increase of both men's substance set the stage for the strife (v.7) and the parting (vv.8-9).

On Lot's wealth, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the occasion: Lot's prosperity, with Abram's (v.2), made their joint dwelling impossible (v.6); the very blessing of increase became the occasion of strife between their herdsmen; the wealth that should have been a benefit brought a difficulty; the setting for Abram's generous parting from Lot (vv.8-9). The verse displays Lot's own wealth in flocks, herds, and tents.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the substance — "Lot also, which went with Abram, had flocks, and herds, and tents," Lot's own wealth (Gill, Henry); the prosperity of Lot alongside Abram (Henry, Gill); Lot's wealth, with Abram's, the occasion of the strife (v.6, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wealthy Lot. The verse marks the Lot: with Abram. The verse displays the flocks: and herds. The verse displays the tents: his. The verse displays the occasion: of strife. The wealth displays Lot prospering alongside Abram. The verse displays the Lot: rich. The verse displays the substance: great. The verse displays the strife: impending. Lot's own wealth in flocks, herds, and tents displays the prosperity that occasioned the parting: Lot also had flocks, herds, and tents — the nephew prospering alongside Abram, the increase of both that would prove too great for the land and bring the strife. The verse displays the wealthy Lot, the land not bearing them both (v.6).
6:
"And the land was not able to bear them, that they might dwell together: for their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together." The brief verse notes that the land could not bear Abram and Lot together. The grammatical voices note the abundance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the land was not able to bear them, that they might dwell together: for their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together" — the land (the pasturage and water available) could not support both Abram's and Lot's great flocks and herds together; their combined substance was too great for the region (especially as the Canaanite and Perizzite also held the land, v.7); the abundance of both made their joint dwelling impossible.

On "the land was not able to bear them," the witnesses note the strain. Gill notes that the land could not bear them — the pasturage was insufficient for both households' livestock; the very increase that God had blessed them with now strained the land; the difficulty of two great pastoral households dwelling in the same limited region.

On "their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the occasion: "their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together" — the greatness of their wealth forced a separation; the blessing of abundance became the occasion of difficulty and (through the herdsmen) of strife (v.7); the need for Abram and Lot to part; the setting for Abram's generous proposal (vv.8-9). The verse displays that the land could not bear Abram and Lot together.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the abundance — "the land was not able to bear them... for their substance was great, so that they could not dwell together," the land insufficient for both households' wealth (Keil, Gill); "the land was not able to bear them," the pasturage too small for both (Gill); "their substance was great," the abundance forcing a separation (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the strained land. The verse marks the land: not able to bear them. The verse displays the substance: great. The verse displays the dwelling: not together. The verse displays the separation: needed. The abundance displays the wealth too great for the land. The verse displays the land: strained. The verse displays the substance: abundant. The verse displays the parting: required. That the land could not bear Abram and Lot together displays the occasion of their separation: the land could not bear them, for their substance was great — the abundance of both households too great for the region, forcing the parting and occasioning the strife. The verse displays the strained land, with the herdsmen's strife (v.7).
7:
"And there was a strife between the herdmen of Abram's cattle and the herdmen of Lot's cattle: and the Canaanite and the Perizzite dwelled then in the land." The witnesses note the strife between the herdsmen and the presence of the Canaanite and Perizzite. On "there was a strife between the herdmen of Abram's cattle and the herdmen of Lot's cattle," the grammatical voices note the quarrel. Ellicott and Gill note that "there was a strife between the herdmen of Abram's cattle and the herdmen of Lot's cattle" — the servants of Abram and Lot quarreled (over pasturage and water, the land being too strait, v.6); the strife between the herdsmen threatened the peace between the households; the occasion of the parting; the contention that Abram would resolve by generous separation (vv.8-9).

On "the herdmen," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the cause. Calvin and Gill note that the strife arose among the herdsmen (over the limited pasture); the contention, if unchecked, would breed division and reproach; the quarrel of the servants endangered the unity of the family; Abram's wisdom and charity would settle it (vv.8-9).

On "the Canaanite and the Perizzite dwelled then in the land," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the witnesses to the strife: "the Canaanite and the Perizzite dwelled then in the land" — the note marks that the heathen inhabitants (Canaanites and Perizzites) were present, watching; the strife between the servants of the godly would be a scandal before the heathen, dishonoring God and the true religion; the presence of the watching pagans made the contention the more grievous, and Abram's peacemaking the more necessary (lest the name of God be reproached). The verse displays the strife between the herdsmen.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the strife — "there was a strife between the herdmen of Abram's cattle and the herdmen of Lot's cattle," the quarrel over pasturage (Ellicott, Gill); the contention endangering the family's unity (Calvin, Gill); "the Canaanite and the Perizzite dwelled then in the land," the watching heathen making the strife a scandal (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the strife. The verse marks the herdsmen: at strife. The verse displays the cause: limited pasture. The verse displays the Canaanite: watching. The verse displays the scandal: before the heathen. The strife displays the herdsmen's quarrel threatening the peace. The verse displays the strife: arisen. The verse displays the heathen: present. The verse displays the peacemaking: needed. The strife between the herdsmen and the presence of the Canaanite and Perizzite display the occasion and danger of the contention: the herdsmen of Abram and Lot strove, and the Canaanite and Perizzite were then in the land — the quarrel over pasturage, made the more grievous and scandalous by the watching heathen, calling for Abram's peacemaking. The verse displays the strife, resolved by Abram's charity (vv.8-9).
8:
"And Abram said unto Lot, Let there be no strife, I pray thee, between me and thee, and between my herdmen and thy herdmen; for we be brethren." The witnesses note Abram's gracious appeal for peace with Lot. On "Let there be no strife, I pray thee, between me and thee," the grammatical voices note the peacemaking. Gill and Clarke note that "Abram said unto Lot, Let there be no strife, I pray thee, between me and thee, and between my herdmen and thy herdmen" — Abram, the elder and the greater, took the initiative to end the strife; he sought peace, deprecating contention between himself and Lot and between their servants; the patriarch's charity and humility, seeking peace rather than standing on his rights; the peacemaker (Matthew 5:9).

On "between me and thee... and between my herdmen and thy herdmen," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the breadth. Calvin and Gill note that Abram sought to remove all strife — between the principals and between the servants; the patriarch, concerned for peace and for the honor of God before the heathen (v.7), would not let the quarrel continue; his wisdom in addressing the root of the contention.

On "for we be brethren," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the bond: "for we be brethren" — Abram appealed to their kinship (uncle and nephew, near kin); brethren ought not to strive; the bond of family and of faith should hold them in peace; the appeal to brotherhood as the ground of concord; Abram's charity, valuing peace and kinship above pasturage and rights. The verse displays Abram's gracious appeal for peace with Lot.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the peacemaking — "Let there be no strife, I pray thee, between me and thee," Abram seeking peace, taking the initiative (Gill, Clarke); "between my herdmen and thy herdmen," the removal of all strife (Calvin, Gill); "for we be brethren," the appeal to kinship as the ground of peace (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the peacemaking Abram. The verse marks the appeal: no strife. The verse displays the herdsmen: at peace. The verse displays the brethren: kinship. The verse displays the charity: of Abram. The appeal displays Abram seeking peace above his rights. The verse displays the Abram: peacemaker. The verse displays the strife: deprecated. The verse displays the brethren: appealed to. Abram's gracious appeal for peace with Lot displays the charity of the peacemaker: let there be no strife between us, for we be brethren — the patriarch, the greater, seeking peace and appealing to kinship, valuing concord above pasturage and his own rights (Matthew 5:9). The verse displays the peacemaking Abram, offering Lot the choice (v.9).
9:
"Is not the whole land before thee? separate thyself, I pray thee, from me: if thou wilt take the left hand, then I will go to the right; or if thou depart to the right hand, then I will go to the left." The witnesses note Abram's generous offer of first choice to Lot. On "Is not the whole land before thee? separate thyself, I pray thee, from me," the grammatical voices note the proposal. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Is not the whole land before thee? separate thyself, I pray thee, from me" — Abram proposed a peaceful separation; the whole land lay open before them, with room enough; Abram urged Lot to part from him, that they might dwell apart and the strife cease; the wise and generous proposal to end the contention by separation.

On "if thou wilt take the left hand, then I will go to the right," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the generosity. Calvin and Gill note that Abram, the elder and the greater (to whom the land was promised), gave Lot the first choice — "if thou wilt take the left hand, then I will go to the right; or if thou depart to the right hand, then I will go to the left"; Abram waived his right of precedence, yielding the choice to his younger kinsman; the remarkable charity and humility of Abram, preferring peace and his nephew's good to his own advantage.

On the offer, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the self-denial: Abram's offer of first choice shows his faith and self-denial — he trusted God for his portion and did not grasp at the best for himself; the man to whom the whole land was promised let his nephew choose first; the generosity that springs from faith (Abram content to take what was left, trusting God's promise); the pattern of charity and peaceableness. The verse displays Abram's generous offer of first choice to Lot.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the proposal — "Is not the whole land before thee? separate thyself, I pray thee, from me," Abram proposing a peaceful separation (Keil, Gill); "if thou wilt take the left hand, then I will go to the right," Abram giving Lot first choice, waiving his precedence (Calvin, Gill); the self-denial and faith of Abram (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the generous Abram. The verse marks the land: before Lot. The verse displays the separation: proposed. The verse displays the choice: given to Lot. The verse displays the charity: of Abram. The offer displays Abram yielding first choice to his nephew. The verse displays the Abram: generous. The verse displays the Lot: to choose. The verse displays the faith: shown. Abram's generous offer of first choice to Lot displays his charity and faith: separate thyself; if thou take the left, I will go right — the patriarch, to whom the land was promised, waiving his precedence and giving his nephew first choice, content to take what was left, trusting God for his portion. The verse displays the generous Abram, as Lot eyes the plain (v.10).
10:
"And Lot lifted up his eyes, and beheld all the plain of Jordan, that it was well watered every where, before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, even as the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt, as thou comest unto Zoar." The witnesses note Lot's worldly choice of the well-watered plain of Jordan. On "Lot lifted up his eyes, and beheld all the plain of Jordan, that it was well watered every where," the grammatical voices note the choice by sight. Ellicott and Gill note that "Lot lifted up his eyes, and beheld all the plain of Jordan, that it was well watered every where" — Lot, given the choice (v.9), looked and saw the plain of Jordan, well watered and fertile; he chose by the sight of his eyes, by what seemed best for his flocks and his prosperity; the choice governed by worldly advantage, not by spiritual considerations.

On "before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, even as the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the deceptive fairness. Henry and Gill note that the plain was, at that time (before God destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah, ch. 19), as fair as "the garden of the LORD (Eden)" or "the land of Egypt" (the well-watered Nile valley); the land looked like paradise; but its fairness was deceptive — it lay under the shadow of coming judgment, and near the wicked cities (v.13); the narrator's note ("before the LORD destroyed Sodom") marks the irony of Lot's choice.

On Lot's choice, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the worldliness: Lot chose by the eye, looking to the richness of the land and his own gain, taking no account of the wickedness of Sodom (v.13) or of the danger to his soul; the choice of the worldly man, walking by sight; the fair appearance masking the spiritual peril; the contrast with Abram, who walked by faith; Lot's choice would lead him toward Sodom and into trouble (ch. 14; 19). The verse displays Lot's worldly choice of the plain of Jordan.

The witnesses agree: the choice — "Lot lifted up his eyes, and beheld all the plain of Jordan, that it was well watered," Lot choosing by sight the fertile plain (Ellicott, Gill); "as the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt," the deceptive fairness, before Sodom's destruction (Henry, Gill); Lot's worldly choice, walking by sight, heedless of Sodom's wickedness (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Lot's worldly choice. The verse marks the eyes: lifted up. The verse displays the plain: well watered. The verse displays the fairness: deceptive. The verse displays the choice: worldly. The choice displays Lot looking to gain, not to his soul. The verse displays the Lot: choosing. The verse displays the plain: fair. The verse displays the peril: unseen. Lot's worldly choice of the plain of Jordan displays the choice by sight: Lot beheld the well-watered plain, fair as Eden or Egypt, and chose it — the worldly man walking by sight, choosing for gain, heedless of Sodom's wickedness and the coming judgment, in contrast to Abram's faith. The verse displays Lot's choice, journeying east (v.11).
11:
"Then Lot chose him all the plain of Jordan; and Lot journeyed east: and they separated themselves the one from the other." The witnesses note Lot's choice of the plain and the separation from Abram. On "Lot chose him all the plain of Jordan," the grammatical voices note the choice. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Lot chose him all the plain of Jordan" — Lot took for himself the whole fertile plain of Jordan (the choicest part, as it then seemed); he chose the best for himself, leaving the rest to Abram; the selfish choice of the worldly man, grasping the apparent advantage.

On "Lot journeyed east," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the move. Clarke and Gill note that "Lot journeyed east" — he moved eastward, toward the plain and the cities (toward Sodom, v.12); the direction of his journey took him toward the wicked cities; the move that would bring him into peril; Lot departing toward the world he had chosen.

On "they separated themselves the one from the other," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the parting: "they separated themselves the one from the other" — Abram and Lot parted, each going his way; the peaceful separation that ended the strife (vv.7-9); the parting of the godly uncle and the worldly nephew; Lot toward Sodom, Abram remaining in Canaan (v.12); the separation that set the course of each man's history. The verse displays Lot's choice of the plain and the separation from Abram.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the choice — "Lot chose him all the plain of Jordan," Lot taking the choicest part for himself (Keil, Ellicott); "Lot journeyed east," his move toward Sodom (v.12, Clarke, Gill); "they separated themselves the one from the other," the peaceful parting of Abram and Lot (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the parting. The verse marks the Lot: choosing the plain. The verse displays the journey: east. The verse displays the separation: from Abram. The verse displays the course: set. The choice displays Lot taking the best and parting from Abram. The verse displays the Lot: departed. The verse displays the plain: chosen. The verse displays the parting: peaceful. Lot's choice of the plain and the separation from Abram display the parting of the worldly and the godly: Lot chose the plain of Jordan, journeyed east, and they separated — the worldly nephew taking the best and moving toward Sodom, the peaceful parting that set the course of each man's history. The verse displays the separated Lot, dwelling toward Sodom (v.12).
12:
"Abram dwelled in the land of Canaan, and Lot dwelled in the cities of the plain, and pitched his tent toward Sodom." The very brief verse notes Abram dwelling in Canaan and Lot pitching toward Sodom. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Gill notes that "Abram dwelled in the land of Canaan, and Lot dwelled in the cities of the plain, and pitched his tent toward Sodom" — Abram remained in the land of Canaan (the land of promise), while Lot dwelt among the cities of the plain, pitching his tent toward Sodom; the contrast between the godly uncle, content in the promised land, and the worldly nephew, drawing near the wicked city.

On "Abram dwelled in the land of Canaan," the witnesses note Abram's portion. Gill notes that Abram dwelt in Canaan — the land God had promised to his seed (12:7); the patriarch, having yielded the choice, remained in the land of promise, content with God's portion; the man of faith abiding where God had placed him.

On "Lot... pitched his tent toward Sodom," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the peril: "pitched his tent toward Sodom" — Lot moved his dwelling toward Sodom, the wicked city (v.13); the gradual drawing near to evil (he would soon dwell in Sodom itself, 14:12; 19:1); the first step of Lot's declension — pitching toward Sodom, then dwelling in it; the danger of choosing nearness to the wicked for worldly gain; the worldly choice leading toward spiritual peril. The verse displays Abram in Canaan and Lot pitching toward Sodom.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "Abram dwelled in the land of Canaan, and Lot dwelled in the cities of the plain, and pitched his tent toward Sodom," the godly uncle in Canaan, the worldly nephew toward Sodom (Gill); "Abram dwelled in the land of Canaan," Abram content in the land of promise (12:7, Gill); "pitched his tent toward Sodom," Lot's first step toward the wicked city (14:12; 19:1, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the contrasted dwellings. The verse marks the Abram: in Canaan. The verse displays the Lot: toward Sodom. The verse displays the promise: Abram's portion. The verse displays the peril: Lot's nearness to evil. The dwellings display the godly and the worldly parted. The verse displays the Abram: abiding. The verse displays the Lot: drawing near Sodom. The verse displays the declension: begun. Abram dwelling in Canaan and Lot pitching toward Sodom display the contrast of the godly and the worldly: Abram dwelt in Canaan, Lot toward Sodom — the man of faith content in the land of promise, the worldly man drawing near the wicked city, the first step of his declension. The verse displays Lot near Sodom, whose men were exceedingly wicked (v.13).
13:
"But the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly." The witnesses note the exceeding wickedness of the men of Sodom. On "the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD," the grammatical voices note the wickedness. Gill and Calvin note that "the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly" — the inhabitants of Sodom, near whom Lot pitched (v.12), were exceedingly wicked; their sin was great and open, "before the LORD" (in his sight, and provoking him); the city ripe for the judgment that would come (ch. 19); the note sets the peril of Lot's choice in sharp relief.

On "wicked and sinners," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the depth of sin. Clarke and Gill note that the men of Sodom were "wicked (toward men, in injustice and violence) and sinners (toward God, in impiety and vice)"; their sin was both against God and against man; the gross and abominable wickedness of Sodom (the sins later named, ch. 19; Ezekiel 16:49-50); the exceeding, ripe wickedness of the city.

On "before the LORD exceedingly," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the provocation and the warning: "before the LORD exceedingly" — their sin was open before God and provoked his wrath; the wickedness so great as to cry to heaven (18:20); the note warns of Lot's danger in choosing to dwell near such a people — for worldly gain he exposed himself and his family to the contagion and the judgment of Sodom (ch. 19); the peril of the worldly choice. The verse displays the exceeding wickedness of the men of Sodom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wickedness — "the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly," the exceeding wickedness of Sodom (Gill, Calvin); "wicked and sinners," their sin against God and man (Ezekiel 16:49-50, Clarke, Gill); "before the LORD exceedingly," their sin provoking God's wrath, the peril of Lot's choice (18:20, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wicked Sodom. The verse marks the men: wicked. The verse displays the sin: exceeding. The verse displays the LORD: before him. The verse displays the peril: Lot's. The wickedness displays Sodom ripe for judgment. The verse displays the Sodom: wicked. The verse displays the sin: great. The verse displays the danger: to Lot. The exceeding wickedness of the men of Sodom displays the peril of Lot's choice: the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly — the city ripe for judgment, near which Lot had chosen to dwell, exposing himself and his family to its contagion and coming doom. The verse displays wicked Sodom, against which the promise to Abram is renewed (vv.14-15).
14:
"And the LORD said unto Abram, after that Lot was separated from him, Lift up now thine eyes, and look from the place where thou art northward, and southward, and eastward, and westward:" The witnesses note the LORD renewing the promise of the land to Abram after Lot's departure. On "the LORD said unto Abram, after that Lot was separated from him," the grammatical voices note the timing. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Abram, after that Lot was separated from him" — God renewed his promise to Abram immediately after Lot had parted from him; the timing is marked — after Abram's generous self-denial (vv.8-9), God came to comfort and reward him; the patriarch, who had yielded the choice to Lot, now received the assurance of God's gift of the whole land.

On "Lift up now thine eyes, and look... northward, and southward, and eastward, and westward," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the survey. Henry and Gill note that God bade Abram "lift up now thine eyes, and look... northward, and southward, and eastward, and westward" — to survey the whole land in every direction; in contrast to Lot, who "lifted up his eyes" (v.10) for his own gain, Abram is bidden by God to lift up his eyes and behold the land God would give him; the whole land, in all directions, promised to Abram and his seed.

On the survey of the land, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the reward of faith: God's renewing the promise after Abram's self-denial shows that the man who yields his rights for peace loses nothing — God gave Abram the whole land, far more than the plain Lot chose; the reward of faith and charity; the comfort of God to his servant; the contrast between Lot's grasping choice and Abram's God-given inheritance. The verse displays the LORD renewing the promise after Lot's departure.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the timing — "the LORD said unto Abram, after that Lot was separated from him," God renewing the promise after Abram's self-denial (Ellicott, Gill); "Lift up now thine eyes, and look... northward, and southward," God bidding Abram survey the whole land (in contrast to Lot, v.10, Henry, Gill); the reward of Abram's faith and charity (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renewed promise. The verse marks the timing: after Lot's parting. The verse displays the eyes: lifted at God's bidding. The verse displays the land: in all directions. The verse displays the reward: of faith. The renewal displays God comforting Abram after his self-denial. The verse displays the LORD: speaking. The verse displays the land: surveyed. The verse displays the promise: renewed. The LORD renewing the promise after Lot's departure displays the reward of Abram's charity: after Lot parted, God bade Abram lift up his eyes and behold the land north, south, east, and west — the comfort and reward of the man who yielded his rights, given the whole land God would bestow. The verse displays the surveying Abram, the land promised for ever (v.15).
15:
"For all the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed for ever." The witnesses note the promise of the land to Abram's seed for ever — the crux of the land-grant. On "all the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it," the grammatical voices note the grant. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "all the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it" — God promised Abram the whole land he beheld (the land of Canaan, in all directions, v.14); the grant of the land to Abram (though he himself possessed none of it, Acts 7:5, but received it in promise) and to his seed; the covenant gift of the land confirmed and enlarged.

On "and to thy seed for ever," interpreters expound the perpetual grant. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the seed and the duration: "to thy seed for ever" — the land was promised to Abram's seed (his posterity, Israel) for ever; the duration "for ever" is variously understood. Some take it of the literal, perpetual possession of Canaan by Abram's natural seed (Israel), to the end of the present age (the land given to that part of his seed which descended through Isaac and Jacob, possessed until the consummation); others read "for ever" as a long but not strictly endless duration (the Hebrew olam meaning a long age, the period appointed). A spiritual/typological reading adds that the land-promise points beyond the earthly Canaan to the heavenly inheritance, the eternal rest of the seed (Hebrews 11:10, 16, Abraham looking for the city with foundations); the earthly land a type of the everlasting inheritance.

On "for ever," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the covenant: the perpetual grant rests on God's covenant faithfulness; whether read of the literal land to Israel for the appointed age, or as pointing to the heavenly inheritance, the promise is sure; the land given to Abram's seed by God's unbreakable word; the covenant of grace embracing both the earthly type and the heavenly substance. The verse displays the promise of the land to Abram's seed for ever.

The witnesses agree the substance: God promised Abram the whole land, to him and his seed for ever (Keil, Gill, Clarke, Calvin). The "for ever" bears a crux: the literal, perpetual possession of Canaan by Abram's natural seed (Israel) for the appointed age vs a long-but-bounded duration (olam) vs a typological pointing beyond earthly Canaan to the heavenly inheritance (Hebrews 11:10, 16). The verse displays the land-grant crux. The verse marks the land: all thou seest. The verse displays the grant: to Abram and his seed. The verse displays the debate: literal Canaan, an age, or the heavenly inheritance. The verse displays the for ever: the duration. The promise displays God granting the land to Abram's seed. The verse displays the land: given. The verse displays the seed: heir. The verse displays the covenant: sure. The promise of the land to Abram's seed for ever displays the covenant grant and a crux: all the land thou seest, to thee and thy seed for ever — read as the literal perpetual possession of Canaan by Israel for the appointed age, or as a bounded "for ever" (olam), or as pointing beyond earthly Canaan to the heavenly inheritance (Hebrews 11:10, 16); the grant sure by God's covenant. The verse displays the granted land, the seed as the dust (v.16).
16:
"And I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth: so that if a man can number the dust of the earth, then shall thy seed also be numbered." The witnesses note the promise of a seed as numberless as the dust of the earth. On "I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth," the grammatical voices note the multitude. Gill and Calvin note that "I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth" — God promised Abram a posterity as numberless as the dust of the earth; the promise of a vast multitude of descendants; to a man whose wife was barren (11:30), God promised an innumerable seed; the human impossibility against which the promise shines.

On "if a man can number the dust of the earth, then shall thy seed also be numbered," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the innumerable multitude. Calvin and Gill note the hyperbole of multitude — "if a man can number the dust of the earth, then shall thy seed also be numbered" — as the dust cannot be counted, so Abram's seed would be beyond numbering; the vast multitude of his descendants (Israel, and the multitude of his spiritual seed, all believers, Romans 4:16-18, Galatians 3:7, 29); the immense fulfillment of the promise.

On the numberless seed, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the faith: the promise of an innumerable seed to a childless old man called for faith; Abram believed God, who quickeneth the dead and calleth things that are not as though they were (Romans 4:17-21); the promise embraced both his natural seed (Israel) and his spiritual seed (all who are of faith, of every nation); the numberless multitude of the faithful, the children of Abraham. The verse displays the promise of a numberless seed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the multitude — "I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth," the numberless posterity promised to a barren man (11:30, Gill, Calvin); "if a man can number the dust... then shall thy seed also be numbered," the innumerable multitude (Israel and the spiritual seed, Romans 4:16-18, Calvin, Gill); the promise calling for faith (Romans 4:17-21, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the numberless seed. The verse marks the seed: as the dust. The verse displays the multitude: innumerable. The verse displays the barren: yet promised. The verse displays the faith: required. The promise displays God's gift of a vast posterity. The verse displays the seed: countless. The verse displays the dust: uncountable. The verse displays the faith: of Abram. The promise of a numberless seed displays God's gift to the childless patriarch: I will make thy seed as the dust of the earth, beyond numbering — the vast multitude of Abram's descendants, natural and spiritual (Romans 4:16-18), promised to a barren man, calling for the faith that believes God who quickens the dead. The verse displays the promised seed, with the call to walk the land (v.17).
17:
"Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it; for I will give it unto thee." The brief verse notes God bidding Abram walk through the land he would give him. The grammatical voices note the command. Gill and Ellicott note that "Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it; for I will give it unto thee" — God bade Abram arise and walk through the land, in its length and breadth; to traverse and survey the land that God would give him; the patriarch invited to take possession, as it were, by walking through it; the symbolic taking of the land in faith.

On "walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the survey. Wesley and Gill note that Abram was to walk through the land, length and breadth — to view and traverse the whole; the act of walking through it a kind of symbolic possession (a customary token of ownership); Abram bidden to make the land his own, in faith, by traversing it; the patriarch taking possession of the promise by walking the land.

On "for I will give it unto thee," the grammatical-historical note the promise: "for I will give it unto thee" — the ground of the command is God's gift; God would give the land to Abram (and his seed, v.15); the patriarch walked through it not as owning it yet (Acts 7:5), but in faith of God's promise; the assurance of the gift confirmed by the invitation to walk through it. The verse displays God bidding Abram walk through the land.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Arise, walk through the land in the length of it and in the breadth of it," God bidding Abram traverse the land (Gill, Ellicott); "walk through the land," the symbolic taking possession by walking it (Wesley, Gill); "for I will give it unto thee," the ground in God's gift (Acts 7:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bidden Abram. The verse marks the command: arise, walk. The verse displays the land: length and breadth. The verse displays the possession: symbolic. The verse displays the gift: God's. The command displays Abram bidden to take the land in faith. The verse displays the Abram: walking. The verse displays the land: traversed. The verse displays the promise: confirmed. God bidding Abram walk through the land displays the symbolic taking of the promise: arise, walk through the land in length and breadth, for I will give it to thee — Abram bidden to traverse and so take possession, in faith, of the land God would give him. The verse displays Abram traversing the land, dwelling at Mamre (v.18).
18:
"Then Abram removed his tent, and came and dwelt in the plain of Mamre, which is in Hebron, and built there an altar unto the LORD." The witnesses note Abram's removal to Mamre in Hebron and his building of an altar. On "Abram removed his tent, and came and dwelt in the plain of Mamre, which is in Hebron," the grammatical voices note the move. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abram removed his tent, and came and dwelt in the plain (oaks/terebinths) of Mamre, which is in Hebron" — Abram moved his tent and settled at Mamre, near Hebron (a place in the south of Canaan); the patriarch, still a tent-dweller (Hebrews 11:9), made Mamre his chief dwelling-place; Hebron, a place of importance in Abram's history (and his burial-place, ch. 23).

On "dwelt in the plain of Mamre," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the settling. Clarke and Gill note that Abram dwelt at Mamre (named from an Amorite ally of Abram, 14:13); the patriarch settled in the oaks of Mamre, his principal residence in Canaan; the place from which much of his later history unfolds (chs. 14; 18); the dwelling of the man of faith in the land of promise.

On "built there an altar unto the LORD," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the worship: "built there an altar unto the LORD" — wherever Abram dwelt, he built an altar and worshiped God (as at Shechem, 12:7, and Bethel, 12:8); the patriarch's habit of worship; the altar at Mamre marking his devotion and his profession of the true God in the land; the man of faith setting up the worship of God in his dwelling-place. The verse displays Abram's removal to Mamre and his building of an altar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the move — "Abram removed his tent, and came and dwelt in the plain of Mamre, which is in Hebron," Abram settling at Mamre near Hebron (Ellicott, Gill); "dwelt in the plain of Mamre," the patriarch's chief dwelling in Canaan (14:13, Clarke, Gill); "built there an altar unto the LORD," Abram's habit of worship at his dwelling (12:7-8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the settled Abram. The verse marks the tent: removed. The verse displays the Mamre: dwelt in. The verse displays the Hebron: its place. The verse displays the altar: built. The settling displays Abram dwelling and worshiping at Mamre. The verse displays the Abram: settled. The verse displays the Mamre: his home. The verse displays the altar: raised. Abram's removal to Mamre and his building of an altar display the dwelling and worship of the patriarch: Abram removed his tent and dwelt at Mamre in Hebron, and built an altar — the tent-dwelling man of faith making Mamre his chief dwelling and setting up the worship of God. The verse closes the chapter, with Abram at Mamre (ch.14).
